paper_title: TaxAgent: How Large Language Model Designs Fiscal Policy
paper_abstract:
Economic inequality is a global challenge, intensifying disparities in
education, healthcare, and social stability. Traditional systems like the U.S.
federal income tax reduce inequality but lack adaptability. Although models
like the Saez Optimal Taxation adjust dynamically, they fail to address
taxpayer heterogeneity and irrational behavior. This study introduces TaxAgent,
a novel integration of large language models (LLMs) with agent-based modeling
(ABM) to design adaptive tax policies. In our macroeconomic simulation,
heterogeneous H-Agents (households) simulate real-world taxpayer behaviors
while the TaxAgent (government) utilizes LLMs to iteratively optimize tax
rates, balancing equity and productivity. Benchmarked against Saez Optimal
Taxation, U.S. federal income taxes, and free markets, TaxAgent achieves
superior equity-efficiency trade-offs. This research offers a novel taxation
solution and a scalable, data-driven framework for fiscal policy evaluation.
related_work_a:
# Related Work

## AI‑Driven Optimal Taxation and Fiscal Policy Design

A first line of work directly targeting adaptive tax‑policy design uses deep reinforcement learning (RL) to co‑train workers and a social planner in an economic simulator. The AI Economist 1 proposes a two‑level deep RL framework in which heterogeneous worker agents and a tax‑setting planner learn concurrently, achieving a ~16% improvement over baselines—including the Saez optimal‑taxation framework—on the equality–productivity trade‑off, while remaining robust to emergent tax‑gaming strategies. Its extended version 2 further formalizes the two‑level RL training procedure and demonstrates that learned tax schedules recover theoretically optimal policies in stylized one‑step economies and outperform them in dynamic multi‑period settings. TaxAI 3 complements these efforts by building a Bewley–Aiyagari‑style dynamic macroeconomic simulator with up to 10,000 heterogeneous households and benchmarking a range of multi‑agent RL algorithms for tax‑policy learning, providing an open testbed for comparing planner strategies. Although these RL‑based approaches address the adaptability limitations of static systems such as the U.S. federal income tax, they still rely on hand‑crafted utility functions and rationality assumptions that our LLM‑based TaxAgent aims to relax.

## LLM‑Powered Generative Agents and Agent‑Based Modeling

A parallel thread reimagines agent‑based modeling (ABM) using large language models to endow agents with richer, more human‑like reasoning. Generative Agents [1] introduce an LLM‑driven architecture combining memory, reflection, and planning to produce believable individual and emergent social behaviors in a sandbox town, establishing the design pattern that many subsequent economic and policy simulators build upon. Surveys of LLM‑based autonomous agents 5 and of LLM‑empowered ABM specifically 6 organize the design space along dimensions such as perception, memory, action generation, and human alignment, motivating our use of LLMs to capture taxpayer heterogeneity and boundedly rational behavior. Concordia 7 further provides a library for constructing generative agent‑based models grounded in physical, social, or digital environments through a Game‑Master architecture, offering an infrastructure blueprint that inspired our macroeconomic simulator. Multi‑agent frameworks such as AgentVerse 8 show that dynamically composed collectives of LLM agents can outperform single agents and give rise to non‑trivial emergent social dynamics, reinforcing the case for multi‑agent designs in policy evaluation.

## LLM Agents for Economic and Social Simulation

Closest to our setting are LLM‑agent simulations of economic and societal phenomena. EconAgent 9 uses LLM‑empowered heterogeneous agents to reproduce classic macroeconomic regularities—Phillips, Okun, and Beveridge relationships—and outperforms rule‑based and RL‑based baselines on the Foundation environment used by prior AI‑Economist work, demonstrating that LLM agents can plausibly model realistic labor and consumption decisions. AucArena [2] evaluates LLM agents' strategic planning and budget management in auction markets, while CompeteAI 11 studies competition dynamics between restaurant and customer agents in a virtual town, yielding micro‑ and macro‑level findings consistent with market theory. Beyond markets, S³ 12 simulates emotion, attitude, and information propagation on real social networks; WarAgent 13 reconstructs historical conflicts to identify triggers of war; and Agent Hospital 14 evolves medical LLM agents through simulated clinical encounters. At larger scale, AgentSociety 15 integrates over ten thousand LLM‑driven agents with a realistic urban environment to study policy interventions such as universal basic income and external shocks, illustrating how generative agents can serve as a testbed for redistributive policies.

## Positioning

Our work sits at the intersection of these lines: like the AI Economist 1, 2 and TaxAI 3, we design adaptive tax rates in a heterogeneous‑agent macroeconomic simulator, but instead of RL policies we drive both households and the government with LLMs following the generative‑agent paradigm [1], 6. By coupling LLM‑based taxpayer heterogeneity 9 with an LLM TaxAgent that iteratively updates tax rates, we obtain a scalable, data‑driven fiscal‑policy laboratory that improves on the Saez benchmark and U.S. federal income tax baselines while accommodating irrational, bounded, and diverse behaviors that prior RL and analytical approaches struggle to capture.
related_work_b:
\subsection{Traditional Tax Systems}
Progressive taxation is relatively simple, imposing higher tax rate on higher income. Empirical studies proved its effectiveness in ameliorating economic inequality~\cite{NBERw21340, NBERw21211} but researchers also pointed out that it lacks adaptability to dynamic economic conditions~\cite{Foo2019ProcessAC, Patjoshi2015DesignAD}.

Optimal taxation explores tax systems that maximize social welfare while accounting for economic constraints and behavioral responses\cite{10.1257/jep.25.4.165}. It can be adjusted according to dynamic economic conditions. Modern frameworks were pioneered by Mirrlees\cite{10.2307/2296779} and Diamond and Mirrlees\cite{RePEc:aea:aecrev:v:61:y:1971:i:1:p:8-27}, aiming to maximize aggregate utility. Saez is one of the largest contributors to this basis. Saez\cite{10.1111/1467-937X.00166} derived optimal nonlinear tax rates by modeling earnings elasticity and income distribution. Diamond and Saez\cite{10.1257/jep.25.4.165} extended this framework, focusing on maximizing social welfare while mitigating income inequality, constructing a closed-loop optimal taxation system.

Economists emphasized the importance of the behavioral responses of taxpayers. Piketty, Saez, and Stantcheva \cite{10.1257/pol.6.1.230} explored the elasticity of the top tax rates and their influence on labor supply and tax avoidance, highlighting the importance of behavioral responses in tax policy design. Kroft, Kucko, Lehmann, and Schmieder \cite{10.1257/pol.20180033} examined how unemployment and wage responses impact tax structures, advocating for the Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC) as a tool to support low-income households while maintaining working incentives.

\subsection{Artificial Intelligence(AI) in Economic Policy Research}
AI offers innovative tools for analyzing and optimizing macroeconomic policies, addressing limitations of traditional models, which rely on equilibrium assumptions. Reinforcement learning and Bayesian Neural Networks enable adaptive simulations and uncertainty quantification. For example, “The AI Economist” framework uses RL to co-adapt agents and social planners\cite{zheng2020aieconomistimprovingequality}. Integration with causal inference techniques further improves policy-impact assessment\cite{NBERc14009}.

ABM captures decentralized decision-making and complex phenomena like systemic risk\cite{AxtellFarmer2022}. ABMs are used to study business cycles, policy interventions, and inflation\cite{DelliGatti2018}. Enhanced computational techniques and high-quality data have improved their empirical validity, enabling applications such as tax policy optimization\cite{zheng2020aieconomistimprovingequality}.

Large Language Models (LLMs) introduce advanced reasoning capabilities to various subjects, including economic research, enabling market behavior simulation and policy evaluation~\cite{shen2025phyxdoesmodelwits,zhao2024competeaiunderstandingcompetitiondynamics, nie2024surveylargelanguagemodels}.

Existing work forms a rule-based framework for optimal taxation and recognizes the impact of taxpayer heterogeneity on optimal taxation design. Nevertheless, current optimal taxation makes rational-man supposition and oversimplified social welfare calculations. In this work, we integrated the advancement in ABMs and LLMs, replaced predetermined rules with LLM-based agents, simulated human-like policy responses, and dynamically adjusted tax rates to generate the optimal social outcome.

paper_title:
KVCache Cache in the Wild: Characterizing and Optimizing KVCache Cache
  at a Large Cloud Provider
paper_abstract:
Serving large language models (LLMs) is important for cloud providers, and
caching intermediate results (KV\$) after processing each request substantially
improves serving throughput and latency. However, there is limited
understanding of how LLM serving benefits from KV\$ caching, where system
design decisions like cache eviction policies are highly workload-dependent. In
this paper, we present the first systematic characterization of the KV\$
workload patterns from one of the leading LLM service providers. We draw
observations that were not covered by previous studies focusing on synthetic
workloads, including: KV\$ reuses are skewed across requests, where reuses
between single-turn requests are equally important as multi-turn requests; the
reuse time and probability are diverse considering all requests, but for a
specific request category, the pattern tends to be predictable; and the overall
cache size required for an ideal cache hit ratio is moderate. Based on the
characterization, we further propose a workload-aware cache eviction policy
that improves the serving performance under real-world traces, especially with
limited cache capacity.
related_work_a:
# Related Work

## LLM Serving Systems and Memory Management
Efficient serving of large language models has become a central systems challenge as cloud providers move billions of requests per day through transformer stacks. vLLM's PagedAttention introduced virtual-memory-style paging of the key–value (KV) cache to eliminate fragmentation and enable dynamic batching, and has become the de-facto backbone of many production stacks 1. vAttention revisited this design and showed that contiguous virtual memory with demand-paged physical backing can match paging benefits while retaining kernel simplicity 2. FastServe attacks head-of-line blocking via token-level preemptive scheduling and a skip-join MLFQ, coupled with GPU–host state migration 3. Sarathi-Serve tames the throughput–latency trade-off through chunked prefills and stall-free scheduling 4. Splitwise 5 and DistServe [1] argue for disaggregating the compute-bound prefill and memory-bound decode phases across pools of accelerators, each of which imposes distinct pressure on KV cache placement and transfer. FlexGen further explores high-throughput single-GPU generation via aggressive offloading of weights and KV state to CPU and disk 7.

## KV Cache Reuse and Prefix Sharing
A large body of work exploits the observation that many requests share prefixes—system prompts, few-shot exemplars, retrieved documents, or prior conversational turns. SGLang's RadixAttention organizes cached KV tensors in a radix tree so that arbitrary common prefixes can be discovered and reused across concurrent programs [2]. Prompt Cache formalizes reusable prompt "modules" via a schema and precomputes their attention states for cross-request reuse [3]. ChunkAttention breaks monolithic KV tensors into chunks indexed by a prefix tree and co-designs a two-phase attention kernel for prefix sharing 10, while Hydragen reformulates attention over shared prefixes as batched matmuls to accelerate large-batch decoding 11. Preble scales prefix reuse to distributed deployments by co-optimizing KV-state locality with load balancing 12. CachedAttention persists per-session KV state across multi-turn conversations, avoiding recomputation of dialogue history 13, and EFIM refines this idea for infilling workloads where reuse boundaries are non-trivial 14. Our study complements these systems by showing, on a real production trace, that single-turn cross-request reuses are as important as multi-turn reuses—an assumption not covered by prior synthetic evaluations.

## Hierarchical and Disaggregated KV Storage
Because on-GPU HBM is scarce, several systems build hierarchical KV pools spanning HBM, host DRAM, SSD, and network. MemServe manages an elastic KV memory pool tailored to prefill/decode disaggregation [4]; Mell extends this to multi-GPU memory-efficient reuse 16. CacheGen encodes KV tensors for compact storage and fast network streaming so that cached context can be shipped between nodes faster than recomputing it 17, and DéjàVu streams KV state for fault-tolerant, pipeline-parallel serving 18. Our findings on the moderate cache size needed for near-ideal hit ratios directly inform the sizing and admission policies for such tiered stores.

## KV Cache Compression and Eviction
Orthogonal to reuse, another line of work reduces the per-request KV footprint. H2O identifies "heavy-hitter" tokens whose keys dominate attention and evicts the rest [5]; SnapKV compresses long-context caches by anticipating which tokens future queries will attend to 20; StreamingLLM shows that retaining a few initial "attention-sink" tokens plus a sliding window preserves quality over unbounded streams [6]. InfiniGen dynamically pages the KV cache with speculative attention estimation 22, while KIVI 23 and Oaken 24 quantize KV tensors to 2–4 bits with online/offline hybrids. These intra-request policies are complementary to the inter-request, workload-aware eviction policy we propose.

## Workload Characterization
Realistic traces are essential for evaluating the systems above. BurstGPT released ten million Azure OpenAI traces and highlighted concurrency and conversation patterns 25, but its focus is request arrivals and lengths rather than KV reuse structure. Our work provides, to our knowledge, the first systematic characterization of KV-cache workload patterns—reuse skew, reuse-time distributions, and category-level predictability—from a leading commercial LLM service, and translates these observations into a workload-aware eviction policy.
related_work_b:
\nospacestitle{Reuse {\kvcache} across requests via {\kvcache} cache. \,}
Reusing {\kvcache} for accelerating LLM serving
has been widely studied~\cite{vllm,chunkattention,cachedattention,promptcache,sglang,cacheblend} and used in commercial LLM serving~\cite{openaiapi,genimiapi,claudeapi,mooncake}.
Currently, production systems only treat cache hits by prefix matches~\cite{chunkattention,sglang,cachedattention,openaiapi,genimiapi,claudeapi,mooncake}, 
because it preserves the original algorithm of model inference with no accuracy loss.
A number of studies~\cite{promptcache,cacheblend,hu2024epic} have studied 
methods for non-prefix {\kvcache} cache. 
We can revisit our characterization once they have matured 
and been deployed in production.

\stitle{Other {\kvcache}-related optimizations. \,}
Emerging works\cite{streamingllm,h2o,infinigen,pyramidkv} propose runtime {\kvcache}
compression and deletion methods to reduce {\kvcache} size.
StreamingLLM~\cite{streamingllm} keeps
only a finite attention window for recent tokens combined with a few initial tokens.
H2O~\cite{h2o} only involves tokens that contribute most to the attention score for inference.
InfiniGen~\cite{infinigen} speculatively selects tokens critical for attention scores and drops others.
KVQuant~\cite{KVQuant} enables 3-bit KV cache quantization with minimal perplexity degradation 
for large language models, achieving fast million-length context inference.
These methods require fewer {\kvcache} per-request,
albeit with accuracy degradation.
Our study is compatible with these works:
if an uncompressed/full {\kvcache} is reusable,
a compressed or deleted version would also be reusable.
%\TODO{add more about the recent sparse attention works.}

%\stitle{Cache in web-service and serverless platform. }
\stitle{Optimizing caching policies. \,}
Optimizing caching has long been studied in the literature,
in general-purpose caching policies~\cite{lruk,slru,twoq,eelru,lrfu,lirs,arc,mq,car,clockpro,DBLP:journals/tos/EinzigerEFM22,lhd,cacheus,sieve,cherkasova1998improving}
or specific domains~\cite{yang2020twemcache,berg2020cachelib,icebreaker,fasscache}.
We continue this line of research,
and leverage our characterized {\kvcache} reuse properties
for optimizing {\kvcache} cache policies.

\stitle{Optimizing LLM serving. \,}
We continue the line of research in optimizing the performance of LLM serving systems~\cite{DBLP:conf/osdi/ZhongLCHZL0024,DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2404-09526,DBLP:conf/sosp/KwonLZ0ZY0ZS23,alpaserve,DBLP:conf/osdi/YuJKKC22,DBLP:conf/isca/PatelCZSGMB24,298501,DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2412-17246},
with a particular focus on characterizing serving workloads
for {\kvcache} cache.
Our work is orthogonal to optimizations other than {\kvcache} cache.
\iffalse
% Cache replacement algorithm is essential to computer systems, and has been researched
% for decades\cite{lruk,slru,lirs,arc,lhd,cacheus,sieve}.
The design of a cache replacement policy needs to take into account the
characteristics of the workload~\cite{lruk,slru,lirs,arc,lhd,cacheus,sieve}.
Therefore, access traces analysis for real-world production systems can help to understand the workload characteristics~\cite{yang2020twemcache,berg2020cachelib,shahrad2020serverless,fasscache,icebreaker}.
% \cite{DBLP:conf/osdi/YangYR20,DBLP:conf/osdi/BergBMGGLUCBHG20} characterize cache workloads in production web-service systems.

Studies of web services~\cite{yang2020twemcache,berg2020cachelib}
have shown that web requests have a Zipf distribution, with bursts of hotspots.
Studies of serverless platforms~\cite{shahrad2020serverless,fasscache,icebreaker} design keep-alive policies for function caching to accelerate function invocations based on the observation that function calling frequencies vary.

paper_title:
Speaking Beyond Language: A Large-Scale Multimodal Dataset for Learning
  Nonverbal Cues from Video-Grounded Dialogues
paper_abstract:
Nonverbal communication is integral to human interaction, with gestures,
facial expressions, and body language conveying critical aspects of intent and
emotion. However, existing large language models (LLMs) fail to effectively
incorporate these nonverbal elements, limiting their capacity to create fully
immersive conversational experiences. We introduce MARS, a multimodal language
model designed to understand and generate nonverbal cues alongside text,
bridging this gap in conversational AI. Our key innovation is VENUS, a
large-scale dataset comprising annotated videos with time-aligned text, facial
expressions, and body language. Leveraging VENUS, we train MARS with a
next-token prediction objective, combining text with vector-quantized nonverbal
representations to achieve multimodal understanding and generation within a
unified framework. Based on various analyses of the VENUS datasets, we validate
its substantial scale and high effectiveness. Our quantitative and qualitative
results demonstrate that MARS successfully generates text and nonverbal
languages, corresponding to conversational input.
related_work_a:
# Related Work

## Co-Speech Gesture Generation

A long line of research has studied how to synthesize body language from speech. Early data-driven methods learned speaker-specific mappings from audio to 2D pose using adversarial training [1](https://arxiv.org/abs/1906.04160), and later extended to 3D conversational gestures learned directly from in-the-wild video [2](https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.06837). Multi-modal context, including text, audio and speaker identity, was subsequently shown to improve naturalness and semantic alignment [3](https://arxiv.org/abs/2009.02119), while hierarchical audio-to-gesture models further captured cross-modal associations at multiple temporal scales [4](https://arxiv.org/abs/2203.13161). Rhythm-aware neural embeddings introduced explicit beat structure into the mapping between speech and motion [5](https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.01448), and denoising diffusion models rapidly became a dominant paradigm for producing diverse, high-quality gestures conditioned on audio [6](https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.09119) or on CLIP-based style prompts [7](https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.14613). The BEAT corpus enabled large-scale semantic and emotional evaluation for such systems [8](https://arxiv.org/abs/2203.05297), and more recent works pursue **holistic** synthesis of face, hands and body: EMAGE unifies expressive masked audio-gesture modeling [9](https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.00374), DiffSHEG jointly generates 3D expression and gesture in real time [10](https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.04747), and ProbTalk focuses on coordinated, variable whole-body motion [11](https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.00368). Our work follows this trajectory toward holistic nonverbal generation, but frames the problem inside a language-model interface rather than as an audio-to-motion regressor.

## Speech-Driven Facial Animation

Nonverbal cues from the face have been extensively studied under the umbrella of talking-face and 3D facial-animation synthesis. VOCA learned subject-agnostic 3D speaking styles from 4D scans [12](https://arxiv.org/abs/1905.03079), MeshTalk disentangled cross-modal audio-face factors for expressive mesh animation [13](https://arxiv.org/abs/2104.08223), and FaceFormer introduced autoregressive transformers to model long-range dependencies in speech-driven animation [14](https://arxiv.org/abs/2112.05329). CodeTalker demonstrated that a **discrete motion prior** learned via vector quantization sharply improves lip-sync realism and codebook-based generation [15](https://arxiv.org/abs/2301.02379) — a design choice that directly inspires the VQ-based nonverbal tokens used in our framework. Emotion-controllable variants such as EmoTalk [16](https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.11089) and EMOTE [17](https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.08990) further disentangle content from affect, while image-space approaches like Wav2Lip [18](https://arxiv.org/abs/2008.10010) and SadTalker [19](https://arxiv.org/abs/2211.12194) produce photo-realistic talking heads from single images. These systems, however, treat text/speech as a one-way conditioning signal and do not model nonverbal cues as part of a bidirectional conversational token stream.

## Multimodal and Motion Language Models

Large language models have been rapidly extended beyond text. LLaMA established strong open foundation models for language [20](https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.13971), which were then adapted to vision (LLaVA) [21](https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.08485) and to video plus audio understanding (Video-LLaMA) [22](https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.02858). In the audio domain, AudioLM cast waveform generation as language modeling over discrete tokens [23](https://arxiv.org/abs/2209.03143), VALL-E showed that neural-codec LMs enable zero-shot text-to-speech [24](https://arxiv.org/abs/2301.02111), and SpeechGPT [25](https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.11000) and AudioPaLM [26](https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.12925) unified speech understanding and generation with textual reasoning in a single decoder. A parallel line treats human motion itself as a "foreign language": T2M-GPT tokenizes motion with a VQ-VAE and generates it autoregressively from text [27](https://arxiv.org/abs/2301.06052), MotionGPT further couples motion tokens with an instruction-tuned LM for multi-task control [28](https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.14795), and AvatarGPT extends this idea to planning and long-horizon motion synthesis [29](https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.16468). MARS builds on this "modality-as-tokens" recipe, but is, to our knowledge, the first to jointly tokenize **text, facial expression and body language** for conversational generation, enabled by the large-scale, time-aligned annotations in the VENUS dataset.
related_work_b:
\noindent \textbf{Multimodal Large Language Models.}
Recent studies have introduced models that combine various modalities with large language models (LLMs), extending their capabilities beyond text to include visual, auditory, and multimodal reasoning. Specifically, to enhance visual comprehension capabilities of LLMs, LLaVA~\cite{liu2024:visual}, Qwen-VL~\cite{bai2023:qwen} and MiniGPT-4~\cite{chen2023:sharegpt4v} have successfully integrated vision encoders into pre-trained LLMs.
Furthermore, VideoChat~\cite{li2023:videochat} and Video-LLaMA~\cite{zhang2023:video} extend these capabilities to video understanding, while models such as Unified-IO-2~\cite{lu2024:unified} and GPT-4-O~\cite{achiam2023:gpt} expand the scope to include auditory modalities, showing robust multimodal reasoning across various inputs.

\noindent \textbf{Learning Dialogue in Video.}
The importance of analyzing conversational sentiment using multimodal data (\textit{e.g.}, text, audio, and visual) from videos has driven the development of numerous datasets~\cite{busso2008:iemocap, zadeh2018:multimodal, poria2019:meld}. 
This has further spurred research into generating and understanding dialogues from videos, leveraging multimodal cues. For instance, Champagne~\cite{han2023:champagne} introduced the YTD-18M dataset for dialogue generation using visual signals and LLMs, while MultiDialog~\cite{park2024:let} combined audio and visual data for generating conversations. Beyond text, efforts like~\cite{shafique2023:nonverbal} and EmotionCLIP~\cite{zhang2023:learning} focus on recognizing nonverbal cues, such as gestures and emotions. Additionally, works like FurChat~\cite{cherakara2023:furchat} and~\cite{lee2023:developing} explore applying nonverbal signals to enhance robotic facial expressions and actions.
However, existing conversational datasets are often limited in scale or fail to include detailed 3D facial and body language information necessary for modeling nonverbal cues effectively. Our VENUS dataset addresses these gaps by being both large-scale and scalable, offering comprehensive conversational data that integrates not only text but also 3D facial expressions and body languages. This enables a more nuanced understanding of nonverbal cues and supports the generation of richer, context-aware conversations.

\noindent \textbf{Human Motion Synthesis in Conversation.}
Recent advancements in 3D human reconstruction~\cite{lin2023:one, dwivedi2024:tokenhmr, danvevcek2022emoca} have significantly improved the quality of pseudo-ground truth data, providing a scalable and accessible alternative to traditional sensor-based methods~\cite{yi2023:generating}.
Leveraging these datasets, recent works~\cite{wu2024:motionllm, lu2023:humantomato} have focused on generating human motions from text.
Building on this progress, our work utilizes pseudo labels derived from our VENUS, which addresses the lack of large-scale dataset for conversational settings. Unlike previous works like~\cite{ng2023:can, ng2022:learning}, which primarily generate listener facial motions from text, our approach extends to produce text, facial expressions, and body language, aligned with conversational context.

paper_title:
Counterfactual Activation Editing for Post-hoc Prosody and
  Mispronunciation Correction in TTS Models
paper_abstract:
Recent advances in Text-to-Speech (TTS) have significantly improved speech
naturalness, increasing the demand for precise prosody control and
mispronunciation correction. Existing approaches for prosody manipulation often
depend on specialized modules or additional training, limiting their capacity
for post-hoc adjustments. Similarly, traditional mispronunciation correction
relies on grapheme-to-phoneme dictionaries, making it less practical in
low-resource settings. We introduce Counterfactual Activation Editing, a
model-agnostic method that manipulates internal representations in a
pre-trained TTS model to achieve post-hoc control of prosody and pronunciation.
Experimental results show that our method effectively adjusts prosodic features
and corrects mispronunciations while preserving synthesis quality. This opens
the door to inference-time refinement of TTS outputs without retraining,
bridging the gap between pre-trained TTS models and editable speech synthesis.
related_work_a:
# Related Work

## Neural Text-to-Speech Synthesis

Modern TTS has evolved from encoder–decoder attention models such as Tacotron 1 and its Transformer variant 2, toward non-autoregressive architectures like FastSpeech 3 and FastSpeech 2 4, and fully end-to-end systems such as VITS 5 and NaturalSpeech 6. More recently, neural-codec language-model approaches — AudioLM 7, VALL-E 8, and SPEAR-TTS 9 — have achieved highly natural, zero-shot speech generation by treating speech as discrete token sequences. While these systems dramatically improve naturalness, they expose only limited external knobs for prosody or pronunciation, motivating post-hoc control techniques such as ours that operate directly on the internal representations of an already-trained model.

## Prosody Modeling and Control

A long line of work has augmented TTS with dedicated prosody modules. Global Style Tokens [1] learn an unsupervised inventory of style embeddings that can be mixed at inference, and reference-encoder–based prosody transfer [2] conditions synthesis on a target utterance to copy its prosodic contour. FastSpeech 2 4 explicitly predicts pitch, energy, and duration so that these variables can be manually overridden, and VITS 5 exposes a stochastic duration predictor for rhythm variation. All of these require training-time architectural commitments: adding a style encoder, prosody predictors, or specific conditioning inputs. In contrast, we seek a mechanism that grants comparable prosodic control over any pretrained model, without retraining or auxiliary modules.

## Pronunciation Modeling and Mispronunciation Correction

Traditional TTS pipelines rely on grapheme-to-phoneme (G2P) front ends 12, 13, typically backed by curated lexicons and phoneme dictionaries. Dict-TTS 14 integrates online dictionaries to disambiguate polyphones, while LRSpeech 15 shows how pronunciation lexicons can be leveraged in extremely low-resource settings. To reduce lexicon dependence, data-driven G2P representations have been proposed for lexicon-free TTS 16. In the assessment literature, mispronunciation detection and diagnosis has been tackled via acoustic-phonetic-linguistic embeddings 17, text-aware end-to-end models 18, and multilingual L1-aware frameworks 19. These approaches presuppose access to reliable phoneme inventories or L2 corpora — a strong assumption we relax by editing model activations to correct specific mispronunciations without touching the front end.

## Activation Editing and Representation Engineering

Our method is inspired by recent work that manipulates hidden states of pretrained models. Causal-mediation analysis 20 and causal abstractions of neural networks 21 established interchange interventions on internal activations as a tool for interpretability. ROME [3] leveraged such interventions to locate and rewrite factual associations directly in the weights of GPT-style models. Building on this, inference-time steering methods — Inference-Time Intervention 23, activation-space steering of language models 24, and Representation Engineering [4] — demonstrate that adding low-dimensional direction vectors to hidden states can control high-level attributes such as truthfulness or style without any gradient updates. To date, these techniques have almost exclusively been studied in the language domain; we transfer this perspective to speech synthesis and show that carefully constructed counterfactual activation edits can serve as controllers for prosody and pronunciation.

## Counterfactual Explanations

Finally, our formulation is influenced by the broader literature on counterfactual reasoning for opaque models. Wachter et al. introduced counterfactual explanations for automated decisions 26, Goyal et al. extended the idea to visual classifiers with counterfactual visual explanations 27, and DiCE explored diverse counterfactuals for tabular predictors 28. We adapt this counterfactual lens to a generative speech model: rather than asking what input change would flip a decision, we ask what minimal activation change would produce a targeted prosodic or phonetic outcome, and use the resulting edit as a controllable, model-agnostic intervention.
related_work_b:
\subsection{TTS Prosody Control}

Text can be spoken in various ways due to semantic nuances, speaking styles, or inherent variability. Traditional approaches, such as unit-selection, capture this variability through speech databases \cite{strom2006expressive}.
In contrast, recent studies model prosodic variation by predicting key prosodic features such as pitch, duration, and energy from their embedding spaces \cite{ren2019fastspeech, ren2020fastspeech, mohan2021ctrl, bandekar2023speaking}, or by using implicit representations learned from a reference encoder to capture the nuanced variations not specified by text alone \cite{wang2018style, skerry2018towards, hsu2018hierarchical}. 

Controlling intermediate representations has also been considered in prior work through the use of embedding bias, calculated by assessing the extent of translation required to achieve specific modifications in acoustic features within multidimensional scaling coordinates \cite{lenglet2022speaking}. However, such modified representations risk deviating from the data manifold, and their application has been primarily confined to controlling duration.

\subsection{TTS Pronunciation Control}

A critical challenge for end-to-end TTS models, which operate without the aid of grapheme-to-phoneme dictionaries or predictive model, is polyphone disambiguation \cite{zhang2020unified}. For TTS models to accurately convert graphemes into phonemes, their linguistic encoder must internalize the varied pronunciation rules, but fully internalizing them is difficult, leading to inevitable pronunciation errors in synthesized speech.

To mitigate mispronunciations, unit selection concatenates recorded speech fragments from a database. However, this often leads to noticeable join artifacts and requires a single-speaker database, limiting the use of non-target speaker data. 

Recently, a model-centric approach called as the Speech Audio Corrector (SAC) \cite{fong2022speech} has been introduced. It leverages speech codes aligned with words, derived from self-supervised learning models to correct mispronunciations at the word level. In contrast, we introduce a model-agnostic approach that manipulates intermediate representations in TTS models.

paper_title: Dual Debiasing for Noisy In-Context Learning for Text Generation
paper_abstract:
In context learning (ICL) relies heavily on high quality demonstrations drawn
from large annotated corpora. Existing approaches detect noisy annotations by
ranking local perplexities, presuming that noisy samples yield higher
perplexities than their clean counterparts. However, this assumption breaks
down when the noise ratio is high and many demonstrations are flawed. We
reexamine the perplexity based paradigm for text generation under noisy
annotations, highlighting two sources of bias in perplexity: the annotation
itself and the domain specific knowledge inherent in large language models
(LLMs). To overcome these biases, we introduce a dual debiasing framework that
uses synthesized neighbors to explicitly correct perplexity estimates, yielding
a robust Sample Cleanliness Score. This metric uncovers absolute sample
cleanliness regardless of the overall corpus noise level. Extensive experiments
demonstrate our method's superior noise detection capabilities and show that
its final ICL performance is comparable to that of a fully clean demonstration
corpus. Moreover, our approach remains robust even when noise ratios are
extremely high.
related_work_a:
# Related Works

**In-Context Learning and Demonstration Selection.** Since large language models were shown to perform tasks from only a handful of in-context demonstrations [1](https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.14165), a large body of work has investigated how the choice of exemplars shapes downstream accuracy [2](https://arxiv.org/abs/2301.00234). Retrieval-based selection was popularized by [3](https://arxiv.org/abs/2101.06804), which picks semantically similar training examples for each test query, and later extended by learned retrievers that are optimized directly for prompt utility [4](https://arxiv.org/abs/2112.08633). Complementary lines of work reduce the annotation budget by choosing a diverse, informative subset to label a priori [5](https://arxiv.org/abs/2209.01975), select prompts via reinforcement learning [6](https://arxiv.org/abs/2211.04486), or compress the corpus from an information-theoretic viewpoint [7](https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.10375). Beyond content, prompt *ordering* itself dramatically affects performance [8](https://arxiv.org/abs/2104.08786), and complementary explanations have been shown to further boost few-shot reasoning [9](https://arxiv.org/abs/2211.13892). All these approaches implicitly assume that the underlying demonstration pool is clean — an assumption that our work explicitly relaxes.

**Role of Labels and Robustness of ICL.** A separate strand of research asks *what* aspects of demonstrations drive ICL. [10](https://arxiv.org/abs/2202.12837) argued that models often exploit format and label-space cues rather than exact input–label correspondence, whereas [11](https://arxiv.org/abs/2205.12685) provided a finer-grained analysis showing that ground-truth labels do matter for many tasks and models. Studies of demonstration robustness under limited or perturbed data further confirm that ICL is sensitive to noisy annotations [12](https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.10693), motivating dedicated methods for handling label corruption in the prompt itself [13](https://arxiv.org/abs/2411.19581). Related few-shot recipes such as LM-BFF [14](https://arxiv.org/abs/2012.15723) and noisy-channel prompting [15](https://arxiv.org/abs/2108.04106) improve stability but do not target explicit noise detection.

**Learning from Noisy Labels.** Robustness to noisy annotations has been studied extensively in classical supervised learning. Neural networks memorize even random labels [16](https://arxiv.org/abs/1611.03530), yet remain surprisingly robust to high noise ratios given sufficient clean signal [17](https://arxiv.org/abs/1705.10694). Practical remedies include Co-teaching between paired networks [18](https://arxiv.org/abs/1804.06872), confident learning that estimates joint label distributions [19](https://arxiv.org/abs/1911.00068), example-forgetting statistics [20](https://arxiv.org/abs/1812.05159), and influence-based data cleansing for SGD [21](https://arxiv.org/abs/1906.08473); see [22](https://arxiv.org/abs/2007.08199) for a survey. In NLP, pretrained language models themselves have been leveraged to detect label errors via loss- or perplexity-based scoring [23](https://arxiv.org/abs/2205.12702), and external LLM guidance has been used to filter noisy fine-tuning data [24](https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.01108). These approaches typically rank samples by *local* likelihood, so — as we demonstrate — their performance degrades sharply once the noise ratio grows large enough to shift the reference distribution.

**Perplexity, Calibration, and Debiasing in LLMs.** Perplexity is the workhorse metric for scoring generations, but recent work shows it is an unreliable proxy for text quality [25](https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.05892) and is systematically biased by prompt format, majority labels, and domain priors baked into the model [26](https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.09690). To counteract such biases, contrastive decoding subtracts an amateur-model log-likelihood from an expert's [27](https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.15097), and DoLa contrasts predictions across transformer layers to reduce hallucination [28](https://arxiv.org/abs/2309.03883). Our dual-debiasing framework is inspired by these ideas: rather than trusting raw perplexity, we synthesize neighboring samples to disentangle annotation bias from the model's intrinsic domain-knowledge bias, yielding a Sample Cleanliness Score that remains reliable even when the corpus is dominated by noise.
related_work_b:
\myparagraph{In-context learning (ICL):} Recent research has leveraged pre-trained LLMs for downstream NLP tasks through in-context learning, particularly in text classification~\citep{yoo2022ground} and generation tasks~\citep{o2023contrastive}. Notable advances include the UDR retriever by \citet{li2023unified}, which works effectively across multiple tasks, and the efficient approach by \citet{liucontext} that extracts in-context vectors from LLM embeddings to reduce computational costs. However, most ICL research assumes clean, high-quality demonstrations, leaving open questions about performance with noisy or imperfect examples.

\myparagraph{ICL with noisy annotations:} Initial studies exploring random labels in ICL classification have shown mixed results. While \citet{min2022rethinking} found limited performance impact with random retrievers for certain LLM-dataset combinations, \citet{yoo2022ground} demonstrated significant performance degradation across a broader range of settings.
More recent work has begun addressing noisy ICL directly. \citet{kang2024context} proposed \textit{Rectification} for classification tasks, though its fine-tuning requirements introduce substantial computational overhead. For generation tasks, \citet{gao2024noise} pioneered the first noise-robust method, but it shows limitations under high-noise conditions.

\myparagraph{Debiasing LLM Output:} Despite their capabilities, LLMs can exhibit biases from their pre-training corpora that impact task performance. To address this, \citet{li2022contrastive} and \citet{zhao2024enhancing} developed Contrastive Decoding, which improves text generation quality by debiasing larger LLMs using outputs from smaller models within the same family. Additionally, \citet{fei2023mitigating} and \citet{zhao2021calibrate} introduced methods to reduce bias in LLMs by addressing both prefixed context bias and finite label bias in classification tasks.

paper_title:
Don't Reinvent the Wheel: Efficient Instruction-Following Text Embedding
  based on Guided Space Transformation
paper_abstract:
In this work, we investigate an important task named instruction-following
text embedding, which generates dynamic text embeddings that adapt to user
instructions, highlighting specific attributes of text. Despite recent
advancements, existing approaches suffer from significant computational
overhead, as they require re-encoding the entire corpus for each new
instruction. To address this challenge, we propose GSTransform, a novel
instruction-following text embedding framework based on Guided Space
Transformation. Our key observation is that instruction-relevant information is
inherently encoded in generic embeddings but remains underutilized. Instead of
repeatedly encoding the corpus for each instruction, GSTransform is a
lightweight transformation mechanism that adapts pre-computed embeddings in
real time to align with user instructions, guided by a small amount of text
data with instruction-focused label annotation. We conduct extensive
experiments on three instruction-awareness downstream tasks across nine
real-world datasets, demonstrating that GSTransform improves
instruction-following text embedding quality over state-of-the-art methods
while achieving dramatic speedups of 6~300x in real-time processing on
large-scale datasets. The source code is available at
https://github.com/YingchaojieFeng/GSTransform.
related_work_a:
# Related Work

## General-purpose Text Embeddings

Learning universal text representations has been a long-standing research goal. Sentence-BERT [1](https://arxiv.org/abs/1908.10084) adapts pretrained BERT with a Siamese network so that sentence-level similarity can be computed efficiently through cosine distance in a shared embedding space. Contrastive objectives were later shown to substantially improve representation quality: SimCSE [2](https://arxiv.org/abs/2104.08821) demonstrated that simple dropout-based positive pairs, together with in-batch negatives, yield strong sentence embeddings, while Sentence-T5 [3](https://arxiv.org/abs/2108.08877) scaled encoder-decoder backbones to produce robust generic sentence encoders. For open-domain retrieval, Dense Passage Retrieval [4](https://arxiv.org/abs/2004.04906) established the dual-encoder paradigm using hard-negative mining, and Contriever [5](https://arxiv.org/abs/2112.09118) extended this direction with fully unsupervised contrastive pretraining. Subsequent work scaled these ideas: GTR [6](https://arxiv.org/abs/2112.07899) showed that very large dual encoders generalize well across retrieval domains, OpenAI's cpt-text embeddings [7](https://arxiv.org/abs/2201.10005) coupled contrastive pretraining with massive weak supervision, and E5 [8](https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.03533) as well as BGE (C-Pack) [9](https://arxiv.org/abs/2309.07597) further combined web-scale weak supervision with high-quality fine-tuning to produce widely used general-purpose encoders. The MTEB benchmark [10](https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.07316) has become the standard evaluation suite for such encoders, revealing however that a single static embedding often cannot capture the many different notions of similarity users may require. Our work follows this line of research but explicitly targets the case in which one representation must be re-shaped on-the-fly to match a user-specified notion of relevance, rather than being learned once and frozen.

## Instruction-following and Task-aware Embeddings

To make embeddings responsive to user intent, INSTRUCTOR [11](https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.09741) proposed prepending natural-language task instructions to each input and training a single encoder over 330 diverse tasks, showing that instructions can steer a shared embedding space toward different downstream objectives. TART [12](https://arxiv.org/abs/2211.09260) extends this idea to retrieval by training a task-aware retriever on a large collection of retrieval datasets paired with human-written instructions, while Promptagator [13](https://arxiv.org/abs/2209.11755) uses a few natural-language prompts to synthesize task-specific queries for training specialized retrievers. More recent LLM-based encoders further blur the line between generation and representation: E5-Mistral [14](https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.00368) fine-tunes a decoder-only LLM on synthetic instruction-conditioned data, GRIT [15](https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.09906) unifies generative and representational instruction tuning inside one model, and NV-Embed [16](https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.17428) introduces architectural and training refinements that push instruction-tuned LLM embedders to state-of-the-art results on MTEB. Promptriever [17](https://arxiv.org/abs/2409.11136) shows that retrievers can be prompted like language models when trained on instance-level instructions. A different line of work reframes instruction following at inference time: InBedder [18](https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.09642) treats each instruction as a question and encodes the model-generated answer as the embedding, obtaining highly instruction-adaptive representations at the cost of per-instruction LLM decoding over the corpus. Dedicated benchmarks such as FollowIR [19](https://arxiv.org/abs/2403.15246) and INSTRUCTIR [20](https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.14334) have shown that many "instruction-aware" retrievers largely react to keywords in the instructions and struggle with genuinely novel user directives, motivating both better training data and more flexible inference-time adaptation.

## Positioning of GSTransform

All of the above instruction-aware approaches share a fundamental cost: whenever the instruction changes, the corpus must be re-encoded by a heavy encoder [11](https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.09741)[12](https://arxiv.org/abs/2211.09260)[14](https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.00368)[15](https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.09906)[16](https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.17428)[18](https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.09642), which is prohibitive on large-scale collections. GSTransform is complementary to these efforts: instead of learning a new encoder or re-encoding the corpus, it operates on top of the strong generic embeddings produced by models such as E5 [8](https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.03533), BGE [9](https://arxiv.org/abs/2309.07597), and their LLM-based successors [14](https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.00368)[16](https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.17428), and learns a lightweight guided space transformation that realigns pre-computed vectors with the user instruction in real time, delivering large speedups while retaining the instruction-awareness targeted by prior work.
related_work_b:
\subsection{Generic Text Embedding}
Text embedding has been a long-studied problem. Since word embeddings, people adopt self-supervised training in generating word embeddings, and pool the word embeddings to form text embeddings \cite{NIPS2013_9aa42b31, pennington-etal-2014-glove}. Recent advancements in context-aware semantic text embedding models leverage Transformer-based architectures \cite{transformer, devlin-etal-2019-bert} as their backbone, often employing customized objectives like contrastive loss to train the models \cite{cer-etal-2018-universal, reimers-gurevych-2019-sentence, gao-etal-2021-simcse, zhuo-etal-2023-whitenedcse}. Moreover, state-of-the-art (SOTA) text embedding models have been further enhanced with techniques such as using large language models (LLMs) \cite{wang2023improving, muennighoff2024generative, lei-etal-2024-meta} and more sophisticated loss functions designed to address issues like cosine saturation \cite{li-li-2024-aoe}. 

Despite their effectiveness, these methods lack generalizability and fail to meet diverse user needs when downstream tasks require focusing on specific aspects beyond general semantics.

\subsection{Instruction-Following Text Embedding}
Instruction-following text embedding~\cite{su-etal-2023-one, peng-etal-2024-answer} allows users to guide embedding generation through customized instructions. 
The model produces embeddings that align with users' specific interests by considering both the input text and instructions.

InstructOR~\cite{su-etal-2023-one} pioneered instruction-based embeddings by concatenating instructions with input texts and training the model using contrastive objectives across a diverse set of instructions. 
It adapts embeddings for varied semantic interpretations but does not explicitly model instruction-specific semantic aspects. 
InBedder~\cite{peng-etal-2024-answer} extends this idea by treating instructions as questions and generating intermediate answers to produce more fine-grained, instruction-aware embeddings. 
They also propose Instruction Awareness Tests, which we adopt to evaluate Triplet Alignment, STS, and Clustering tasks.
%%%
Yet, both methods require re-encoding the entire corpus for each new instruction, resulting in notable computational overhead and latency, especially for large-scale datasets.

Beyond text embeddings, related efforts have explored instruction-aware and prompt-based information retrieval~\cite{weller2024promptriever, min2024unihgkr, oh2024instructir, sun2024mair, weller2024followir}, offering alternative formulations that leverage user intent to enhance retrieval quality.

paper_title: NexusSum: Hierarchical LLM Agents for Long-Form Narrative Summarization
paper_abstract:
Summarizing long-form narratives--such as books, movies, and TV
scripts--requires capturing intricate plotlines, character interactions, and
thematic coherence, a task that remains challenging for existing LLMs. We
introduce NexusSum, a multi-agent LLM framework for narrative summarization
that processes long-form text through a structured, sequential
pipeline--without requiring fine-tuning. Our approach introduces two key
innovations: (1) Dialogue-to-Description Transformation: A narrative-specific
preprocessing method that standardizes character dialogue and descriptive text
into a unified format, improving coherence. (2) Hierarchical Multi-LLM
Summarization: A structured summarization pipeline that optimizes chunk
processing and controls output length for accurate, high-quality summaries. Our
method establishes a new state-of-the-art in narrative summarization, achieving
up to a 30.0% improvement in BERTScore (F1) across books, movies, and TV
scripts. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of multi-agent LLMs in
handling long-form content, offering a scalable approach for structured
summarization in diverse storytelling domains.
related_work_a:
# Related Work

Summarizing long-form narratives such as novels, movie scripts, and TV shows sits at the intersection of three active research threads: narrative-specific summarization, long-context modeling, and multi-agent LLM systems. We review each area and situate NexusSum within it.

## Narrative Summarization

A first line of work has focused on building resources and models tailored to story-like inputs. BookSum 1 provides multi-granularity summaries of full books and chapters, while MovieSum 2 and Select-and-Summarize (MENSA) 3 target long screenplays that mix dialogue with stage directions. SummScreen 4 focuses on abstractive summarization of TV transcripts, and NarraSum 5 offers a large-scale corpus of general narrative summaries. These benchmarks all expose the same difficulties that motivate NexusSum: extremely long inputs, evolving character arcs, and the interleaving of prose and dialogue. Early modeling efforts adopt an extract-then-abstract paradigm, either selecting salient chapters or scenes 6 or filtering by scene saliency classifiers 3, but these pipelines routinely drop character interactions that are critical for coherence. Dialogue-heavy inputs have also inspired preprocessing tricks such as speaker normalization and bag-of-tricks strategies 7, which anticipate our Dialogue-to-Description Transformation but do not scale to book-length narratives.

## Long-Context Summarization

A second line of work modifies transformer architectures so that a single model can ingest tens of thousands of tokens. Sparse-attention models such as Longformer 8, Big Bird 9, Reformer 10, and Linformer 11 reduce the quadratic cost of self-attention, while LongT5 12 combines transient global attention with denoising pre-training for long-input generation. Positional-interpolation techniques 13 further extend the effective context of pretrained LLMs. However, empirical studies show that even models with 100K+ token contexts suffer from the "lost in the middle" phenomenon and degrade sharply on multi-turn dependencies, entity tracking, and thematic continuity [1]. Chunk-based methods sidestep hard length limits by segmenting the document: SLED 15 fuses short-encoder passes over overlapping windows, Unlimiformer 16 augments a transformer decoder with an unbounded retrieval index over encoder states, and CachED 17 uses gradient caching for encoder-decoder fine-tuning. Multi-stage extractive-abstractive frameworks such as SummN generalize this idea by recursively summarizing coarse-to-fine partitions. All of these approaches require fine-tuning or architectural modifications, whereas NexusSum operates in a training-free regime on top of off-the-shelf LLMs.

## Multi-Agent LLMs for Long-Form Summarization

A third and more recent line of work coordinates multiple LLM calls as cooperating agents. A survey of LLM-based multi-agent systems [2] documents the rapid diversification of role-based, hierarchical, and refinement pipelines. Directly relevant to summarization, BooookScore 19 systematically studies hierarchical merging and incremental updating for book-length inputs, showing that chunked LLM pipelines can rival fine-tuned specialists on coherence metrics. Chain of Agents 20 formalizes a multi-agent pipeline in which each worker refines a rolling summary passed sequentially along the chunks. Agent-as-Judge frameworks combine hierarchical summarization with factual refinement over character knowledge graphs 21. Beyond pure summarization, retrieval-augmented generation [3] improves factuality by grounding outputs in retrieved passages, though it struggles with the thematic continuity required for storytelling. These systems demonstrate the promise of coordinating specialized LLM agents but treat documents largely as generic text; they do not exploit narrative-specific structure such as dialogue attribution, scene boundaries, or controllable output length.

## Positioning of NexusSum

Complementing prior evidence that modern zero-shot LLMs still trail task-specific pipelines on abstractive summarization benchmarks 23, NexusSum bridges the three threads above. Like narrative-summarization work, it explicitly models the prose/dialogue duality via a Dialogue-to-Description Transformation. Like long-context methods, it segments arbitrarily long inputs into scene-based chunks, but avoids architectural changes or fine-tuning. Like multi-agent frameworks such as Chain of Agents 20, BooookScore 19, and hierarchical merge-and-refine agents 21, it decomposes summarization into cooperating LLM roles, but adds an Iterative Compression agent that provides fine-grained length control—an aspect largely absent from prior multi-agent summarization pipelines. Together these choices yield state-of-the-art BERTScore on BookSum 1, MovieSum 2, MENSA 3, and SummScreen 4, while remaining fine-tuning-free.

paper_title: COSMIC: Generalized Refusal Direction Identification in LLM Activations
paper_abstract:
Large Language Models (LLMs) encode behaviors such as refusal within their
activation space, yet identifying these behaviors remains a significant
challenge. Existing methods often rely on predefined refusal templates
detectable in output tokens or require manual analysis. We introduce
\textbf{COSMIC} (Cosine Similarity Metrics for Inversion of Concepts), an
automated framework for direction selection that identifies viable steering
directions and target layers using cosine similarity - entirely independent of
model outputs. COSMIC achieves steering performance comparable to prior methods
without requiring assumptions about a model's refusal behavior, such as the
presence of specific refusal tokens. It reliably identifies refusal directions
in adversarial settings and weakly aligned models, and is capable of steering
such models toward safer behavior with minimal increase in false refusals,
demonstrating robustness across a wide range of alignment conditions.
related_work_a:
# Related Work

**Linear representations and probing of concepts.** A long line of work has shown that neural networks encode high-level concepts as approximately linear directions in their internal activation space. Early probing methods trained linear classifiers on intermediate features to expose the information carried by each layer 1. More recently, Burns et al. demonstrated that latent "truth" directions can be recovered without labels using contrast-consistent search 2, while Park et al. formalized the *linear representation hypothesis* and characterized the geometry of concept subspaces in large language models 3. Sparse autoencoders have been proposed as a scalable way to discover monosemantic feature directions in the residual stream [1]. COSMIC builds directly on this linear-concept view: rather than probing for interpretability, it selects and validates concept directions using cosine similarity in activation space.

**Activation steering and representation engineering.** A growing body of work manipulates model behavior by directly editing hidden activations. Inference-Time Intervention (ITI) shifts attention-head activations along truthfulness directions computed from labeled contrast pairs 5. ActAdd (Activation Addition) constructs steering vectors from a single pair of contrasting prompts and adds them at inference time 6, while Contrastive Activation Addition (CAA) averages differences over many behavioral contrast pairs to steer Llama-2 on behaviors such as sycophancy and refusal 7. Representation Engineering (RepE) provides a broader top-down framework for reading and controlling concepts including honesty, morality, and power-seeking [2]. In-Context Vectors likewise distill task demonstrations into latent-space steering signals 9, and recent work aims for universal steering and monitoring across models and behaviors 10. Most of these techniques rely on labeled contrast pairs and select layers or directions by measuring downstream output changes; COSMIC removes the need for this output-based supervision by using purely internal cosine-similarity criteria.

**Refusal directions and safety-relevant behaviors.** Closest to our work, Arditi et al. showed that refusal in a wide range of aligned chat models is mediated by a single one-dimensional subspace, and that erasing this direction disables refusal while adding it induces over-refusal 11. Their pipeline, however, presupposes access to detectable refusal templates in the output and selects layers by evaluating refusal string frequencies. Trojan Activation Attacks exploit similar steering mechanics for red-teaming safety-aligned models 12. COSMIC generalizes this line of work by identifying viable refusal directions and target layers without any assumption about refusal-token patterns, enabling reliable steering even for weakly aligned or adversarially perturbed models.

**Jailbreaks and adversarial alignment.** Understanding refusal directions is tightly connected to jailbreak research. Wei et al. analyzed how safety training fails through competing objectives and mismatched generalization 13. Zou et al. introduced the GCG suffix attack, an optimization-based universal jailbreak transferable across aligned LLMs 14. Follow-up work shows that even leading safety-aligned models fall to simple adaptive attacks [3] and to persuasive natural-language prompts 16. In-context demonstrations have also been used both to jailbreak and to guard aligned models [4]. These attacks motivate COSMIC's evaluation in adversarial settings where output-based direction selection is unreliable because refusals are suppressed or disguised.

**Benchmarks for steering.** Recent benchmarks such as AxBench systematically compare steering methods and show that simple activation-based baselines can be highly competitive with more complex approaches 18. COSMIC contributes to this line by providing an automated, output-independent direction-selection procedure that matches prior steering performance while broadening applicability to models whose refusal behavior cannot be characterized a priori.
related_work_b:
Our work builds on research in LLM safety and mechanistic interpretability.
\paragraph{Safety:} LLM alignment is typically achieved through fine-tuning \cite{ouyang2022traininglanguagemodelsfollow} and RLHF \cite{bai2022traininghelpfulharmlessassistant, ganguli2022redteaminglanguagemodels}, yet studies show that fine-tuning \cite{lermen2024lorafinetuningefficientlyundoes, yang2023shadowalignmenteasesubverting, qi2023finetuningalignedlanguagemodels} and adversarial prompts \cite{andriushchenko2024jailbreaking, zou2023universaltransferableadversarialattacks, chao2024jailbreakingblackboxlarge} can bypass refusal mechanisms. 
\paragraph{Steering:} Recent work demonstrate refusal behavior is encoded in activation space \cite{weidinger2021ethicalsocialrisksharm, arditi2024refusallanguagemodelsmediated, marshall2024refusalllmsaffinefunction} with interventions aiming to modulate it directly \cite{zou2023representationengineeringtopdownapproach, arditi2024refusallanguagemodelsmediated, marshall2024refusalllmsaffinefunction, Spectralediting, bhattacharjee2024inferencetimecategorywisesafetysteering, uppaal2025profs}. 
Many methods use contrastive data pairs to extract feature directions \cite{burns2024discoveringlatentknowledgelanguage, arditi2024refusallanguagemodelsmediated, panickssery2024steeringllama2contrastive, zou2023representationengineeringtopdownapproach} for behavior steering \cite{zou2023representationengineeringtopdownapproach, panickssery2024steeringllama2contrastive, turner2024steeringlanguagemodelsactivation, arditi2024refusallanguagemodelsmediated, lee2025programmingrefusalconditionalactivation} and concept removal techniques \cite{guerner2024geometricnotioncausalprobing, haghighatkhah2022betterhitnailhead, ravfogel2020nulloutguardingprotected, belrose2023leaceperfectlinearconcept} such as Representation Engineering and Contrastive Activation Addition \cite{zou2023representationengineeringtopdownapproach, panickssery2024steeringllama2contrastive}.\citet{wang2024trojanactivationattackredteaming} also uses similarity-based scores to target intervention layers.

\paragraph{Interpretability}: Model behaviors are often represented as linearly encoded in activation space \cite{bolukbasi2016man, elhage2022toymodelssuperposition, park2024linearrepresentationhypothesisgeometry, mikolov2013linguistic, nanda2023emergentlinearrepresentationsworld, hernandez2021lowdimensionallineargeometrycontextualized}, although other work posit refusal behaviors as affine functions \cite{marshall2024refusalllmsaffinefunction}. These hypothesis are investigated via mechanistic interpretability approaches leveraging sparse autoencoders \cite{bricken2023monosemanticity, templeton2024scaling, cunningham2023sparseautoencodershighlyinterpretable}, weight-based analysis \cite{pearce2024bilinearmlpsenableweightbased}, and circuit analysis \cite{elhage2021mathematical, lieberum2023doescircuitanalysisinterpretability} to further understand model internals.

paper_title:
Is Your Model Fairly Certain? Uncertainty-Aware Fairness Evaluation for
  LLMs
paper_abstract:
The recent rapid adoption of large language models (LLMs) highlights the
critical need for benchmarking their fairness. Conventional fairness metrics,
which focus on discrete accuracy-based evaluations (i.e., prediction
correctness), fail to capture the implicit impact of model uncertainty (e.g.,
higher model confidence about one group over another despite similar accuracy).
To address this limitation, we propose an uncertainty-aware fairness metric,
UCerF, to enable a fine-grained evaluation of model fairness that is more
reflective of the internal bias in model decisions compared to conventional
fairness measures. Furthermore, observing data size, diversity, and clarity
issues in current datasets, we introduce a new gender-occupation fairness
evaluation dataset with 31,756 samples for co-reference resolution, offering a
more diverse and suitable dataset for evaluating modern LLMs. We establish a
benchmark, using our metric and dataset, and apply it to evaluate the behavior
of ten open-source LLMs. For example, Mistral-7B exhibits suboptimal fairness
due to high confidence in incorrect predictions, a detail overlooked by
Equalized Odds but captured by UCerF. Overall, our proposed LLM benchmark,
which evaluates fairness with uncertainty awareness, paves the way for
developing more transparent and accountable AI systems.
related_work_a:
# Related Works

## Fairness and Bias in NLP Systems

Early studies of bias in NLP focused on distributional word representations, showing that embeddings learned from large corpora encode strong gender stereotypes and reproduce human implicit associations 1, 2. Subsequent work demonstrated that these biases propagate into downstream applications: contextualized embeddings such as ELMo unequally encode gender information for male and female entities and induce measurable bias in coreference systems that depend on them 3. Broader critical surveys have questioned the conceptual grounding of "bias" in NLP research and called for clearer normative framings and stronger alignment between measurement and social harm 4, while general machine-learning surveys have catalogued the space of algorithmic fairness definitions and their trade-offs 5. Classical algorithmic-fairness criteria such as Equalized Odds and Equality of Opportunity operationalize fairness as parity of accuracy-based error rates across sensitive groups 6; our proposed UCerF metric is motivated by the observation that such purely accuracy-based criteria do not capture confidence disparities.

## Gender–Occupation Bias in Coreference Resolution

The most direct antecedents of our dataset are the WinoBias and Winogender benchmarks, which use Winograd-schema-style minimal pairs of sentences differing only in gendered pronouns to probe stereotype-driven coreference decisions 7, 8. These datasets revealed that coreference systems disproportionately link gendered pronouns to occupations aligned with real-world gender statistics. Follow-up work applied causal mediation analysis to localize the components of neural language models responsible for these gender–occupation associations 9. Our benchmark scales this line of inquiry, providing 31,756 gender–occupation coreference samples that address the size, diversity, and clarity limitations of prior Winograd-style resources.

## Bias Evaluation Benchmarks and Metrics for LLMs

A growing family of benchmarks measures bias in large language models along complementary axes. StereoSet 10 and CrowS-Pairs 11 probe stereotypical associations through masked-language-model scoring, while BBQ evaluates social biases in question answering 12, BOLD studies open-ended generation across demographic prompts 13, and RealToxicityPrompts examines toxic degeneration 14. Regard-based analysis quantifies polarity disparities toward demographic groups in generated text 15. Broader trust and holistic evaluation suites embed fairness alongside robustness, calibration, and privacy 16, 17. Recent surveys consolidate this rapidly growing literature and taxonomize evaluation metrics, datasets, and mitigation techniques for LLM fairness 18, 19. Almost all of these benchmarks, however, reduce fairness to differences in discrete accuracy or generation statistics, ignoring the distributional information carried by model confidence.

## Uncertainty Quantification and Calibration

The neural-network community has long recognized that a model's predicted probabilities carry information beyond top-1 accuracy. Modern deep networks are known to be miscalibrated, motivating post-hoc calibration techniques such as temperature scaling [1]. Bayesian approximations via dropout 21 and deep ensembles 22 provide scalable predictive-uncertainty estimates. For LLMs specifically, empirical studies have examined how uncertainty behaves in pre-trained language models across tasks 23, whether models "know what they know" through self-evaluation and P(True) style probing 24, and how semantic equivalence of generated outputs can be aggregated into semantic entropy 25. Our UCerF metric builds on this line of work by combining group-conditional confidence with correctness to expose bias that is hidden from accuracy-only fairness measures — for example, high confidence in incorrect predictions concentrated in one demographic group.

## Modern Open-Source LLMs Under Evaluation

The models we benchmark span the current generation of open-weights LLMs, whose few-shot and instruction-following abilities have grown rapidly since GPT-3 [2]. Representative examples include LLaMA 27 and Mistral-7B [3], for which we show that conventional accuracy-based fairness diagnostics miss critical confidence-driven biases that UCerF surfaces.

% [Oliver] TODO: make shorter
\subsection{Fairness Evaluation of LLMs}
LLM evaluation has become a critical research area~\cite{liang2022holistic,hendrycks2020measuring,open-llm-leaderboard-v2}, especially the safety of LLMs~\cite{blodgett-etal-2020-language}. While efforts have been made in fields such as adversarial robustness~\cite{yang2024assessing}, toxicity~\cite{hartvigsen2022toxigen}, and harmfulness~\cite{magooda2023framework}, gender fairness in LLMs remains an important area of research that requires further exploration and development~\cite{li2023survey,mackraz2024evaluating,patel2024fairness}.

Currently, most model fairness evaluations focus on prediction-based metrics (i.e., whether the model predictions are correct and/or unbiased)~\cite{laskar2023systematic,chu2024fairness}. 
For example, demographic parity quantifies the difference in positive prediction rates across demographic groups, while equalized odds measures the difference in error.

Other metrics propose first collecting generated responses from various tasks (e.g., continuation of input text) and analyzing the generation quality (e.g., presence of bias) as indirect reflections of fairness~\cite{wang2024ceb}. 
Nonetheless, existing fairness metrics do not consider model uncertainty, which contains information about a model's internal decision-making~\cite{ye2024benchmarking} and can influence fairness estimation.

Besides metrics, fairness evaluation datasets are another cornerstone for measuring LLM fairness~\cite{fabris2022algorithmic}. However, existing datasets to assess gender-occupation bias in LLMs have limitations. Datasets based on the WinoGrad Schema~\cite{levesque2012winograd}, such as WinoBias~\cite{zhao2018gender}, WinoBias+~\cite{vanmassenhove2021neutral} and WinoGender~\cite{rudinger2018gender} are no longer adequate to evaluate recent LLMs as detailed in~\cref{sec:dataset}. Datasets like Big-Bench (the Disambiguation\_QA task)~\cite{srivastava2023beyond}, BOLD~\cite{dhamala2021bold}, a variation of WinoBias~\cite{kotek2023gender}, and BBQ (the gender-identity split)~\cite{parrish2021bbq} either suffer from limited size or are based on templates which limit the diversity of sentence syntax and context. GAP~\cite{webster-etal-2018-mind} and GAP-Subjective~\cite{pant-dadu-2022-incorporating} focus on pronoun-name bias instead of occupation, which is not relevant to our work. BUG~\cite{levy-etal-2021-collecting-large}, which scrapes large-scale real-world corpus using 14 fixed searching patterns, suffers from limited syntax that could be memorized by models and label noise. To overcome existing limitations in studying gender-occupation biases, we use a state-of-the-art LLM to generate SynthBias, a large-scale, high-quality, and diverse synthetic dataset representing varied freeform contexts.

\subsection{Uncertainty Estimation of LLMs}
Model uncertainty~\cite{gawlikowski2023survey} is a critical factor in evaluating the reliability of language models~\cite{hu2023uncertainty,huang2023look,fadeeva2023lm,ye2024benchmarking,kendall2017uncertainties,ye2024benchmarking}. 
To quantify the model uncertainty of LLMs, estimation methods can be categorized~\cite{hu2023uncertainty} as: 
(1) Confidence-based methods, i.e., aggregating model prediction confidence, such as softmax response~\cite{bridle1990probabilistic,hendrycks2017a}, perplexity~\cite{jurafsky2000speech}, softmax-entropy-based approaches~\cite{fomicheva2020unsupervised,malinin2021uncertainty}, and conformal prediction~\cite{vovk2005algorithmic}; 
(2) Sampling-based methods, i.e., estimating uncertainty through repeated sampling, such as MC Dropout~\cite{gal2016dropout}, mutual information~\cite{yu2022learning}, predictive-entropy-based approaches~\cite{malinin2021uncertainty,kuhn2023semantic,duan2023shifting}, and P(True)~\cite{kadavath2022language}; 
(3) Distribution-based methods, i.e., modeling uncertainty by parameterizing probability distributions, such as prior networks~\cite{malinin2018predictive} or divergence and distance~\cite{darrin2022rainproof}. As recent studies~\cite{vashurin2025benchmarking,santilli2024spurious,santilli2025revisiting} show that logit-based uncertainty estimators can be as effective as more recent uncertainty metrics, in this paper, we use perplexity to estimate model uncertainty for its simplicity and intuitive interpretability.

\subsection{Uncertainty-Aware Fairness}
While fairness evaluation and uncertainty estimation of LLMs have been separately studied, the intersection of the two areas is overlooked despite its value~\cite{mehta2024evaluating}. The importance of evaluating models on both fairness and reliability (uncertainty) metrics is shown in~\cite{kuzmin-etal-2023-uncertainty}, but this work studies fairness and reliability as two separate metrics and focuses on how debiasing methods impact the trade-off between the two metrics. Distinctively, our work promotes an improvement in the fairness metric itself by incorporating uncertainty information. Uncertainty analysis reveals additional insights into model behavior, helping to uncover subtle fairness differences that conventional metrics may overlook~\cite{kuzucu2023uncertainty,kaiser2022uncertainty,liang2022holistic}. 

By incorporating uncertainty estimation, previous methods~\cite{kaiser2022uncertainty,tahir2023fairness} have gained additional information and achieved fair model outcomes in the decision-making process. However, these methods are designed for tabular and vision datasets. In the specific area of LLM fairness, to the best of our knowledge, only one work~\cite{kuzucu2023uncertainty} has considered uncertainty by evaluating whether two groups have the same uncertainty, complementing conventional group fairness metrics. However, this method does not jointly consider uncertainty and correctness, neglecting complex scenarios with varying correctness and uncertainty levels, which is essential for comprehensive fairness evaluation as shown in~\cref{sec:metric}.

paper_title: Pre-Training Curriculum for Multi-Token Prediction in Language Models
paper_abstract:
Multi-token prediction (MTP) is a recently proposed pre-training objective
for language models. Rather than predicting only the next token (NTP), MTP
predicts the next $k$ tokens at each prediction step, using multiple prediction
heads. MTP has shown promise in improving downstream performance, inference
speed, and training efficiency, particularly for large models. However, prior
work has shown that smaller language models (SLMs) struggle with the MTP
objective. To address this, we propose a curriculum learning strategy for MTP
training, exploring two variants: a forward curriculum, which gradually
increases the complexity of the pre-training objective from NTP to MTP, and a
reverse curriculum, which does the opposite. Our experiments show that the
forward curriculum enables SLMs to better leverage the MTP objective during
pre-training, improving downstream NTP performance and generative output
quality, while retaining the benefits of self-speculative decoding. The reverse
curriculum achieves stronger NTP performance and output quality, but fails to
provide any self-speculative decoding benefits.
related_work_a:
# Related Work
